hypothesis: At the age of 12, it was the first time I had to face myself.
It was the year I started sports and changed schools.
I was now a student at the city's music school and completely in my teenage years.
My classmates were talented children with various hobbies.
Others played music, others played soccer, and others were simply better than me.
Or at least that's how I felt.
I felt that I was falling behind in many areas compared to my peers and compared to what I had in my mind about what I could be and become in the future.
Think about it, I was overweight, awkward, and not at all popular.
I believed that this situation was not deserved by me, so I started looking for ways to change it.
My first move was to search on Google, as we all do nowadays, about how to become better, smarter, how I could make a good impression in a group, and I continued doing all sorts of related searches.
I started reading self-improvement books and articles daily.
At some point, I believed that a very good choice, apart from articles on the internet, would also be a book that would contain condensed knowledge for me.
I was learning about techniques and methods that could help me improve, and I was trying to apply them.
I studied body language, tone of voice, speech speed, and aimed to control the tone of each discussion.

Well, as you can understand, it was very difficult to do all of this from scratch, suddenly, and to study all of these things at the same time, and it was unrealistic to believe that I would succeed so easily.

That's how I realized that theory was far removed from practice, and around there I started to believe that I would never reach where I wanted to go, with disappointment growing more and more on my face.
And then, an event was about to change my entire theory about life, and many things, at least as I perceived them about myself.
It was a simple suggestion from a teacher at my school, to participate in a big short film competition.
We participated and stood out among the best teams in the competition, and that was the moment I realized something very important.
The next day after our success, I asked myself a few questions.

What was it that made me feel happiness and relief when I succeeded? Why do I believe I cannot chase my dreams? Why should I fear losing something I never had, and why does the fear of failure overshadow the fear of success? What is it that I truly seek from my life, and what is my purpose? Strange things happen around us every day.
Millions of people are born and die at the same time, without us having any idea that we coexist or have coexisted in the past with them for a period of time.
Billions of people have completely different ideas, perceptions, and beliefs from us.
And there are these people we consider rare and unique, and we are happy and glad that they are by our side.
However, they and the rest and all of us have a common element.
This period between birth and death that we call life.
Life, as you all know, is not just play and laughter, right? Wrong!
Life is to play, laugh, cry, fight, take risks, win, and lose.
Life, therefore, is a very big game and has many parameters and many rules.
We know some of their rules from the beginning, and we learn other parameters and rules later by playing this game.
An essential part of the game is to define your own framework and your own rules from the beginning so that you can reach the desired outcome.
But what is the right path that will lead you to the goal? Most likely, there is no single right path.
And if the path you have chosen leads you to your goal, then your reward comes as well.
You see, games are connected and have been influenced by life.

Shouldn't this also happen in our lives? Many times we have thought that this is our last chance to succeed, we live with deadlines set by others for us, and this makes us fear failure, leading us to often act wrongly or sometimes not to act at all.
Imagine now how our lives would be if they had been affected by games.
First of all, there would be no fear of failure, because we all know that in games you always have a second chance.
Even when the lives and chances in your game are decreasing, you are able to increase them and make life and the game last longer.
After all, what's the reason not to do the same in our lives? Every time we fail at something, we automatically increase our success rate in the next attempt.
Another important element we often notice in games is time.
If you've noticed, usually, many games don't become harder but faster.
For example, today, here, I could give a better speech in my own game, yet the TEDx organizers have given me a specific time to speak, making the game faster for me, not harder.
Since, therefore, we know that our time is limited in this game and the hourglass turns only once, we set our own level of difficulty in relation to it.
Most of the time we judge that it's either too difficult for us or that we don't have time.
We are bound by the same thing, considering it in two different ways.
The way to escape this is to have a clear purpose in our game and, by extension, in our lives.
It is that hourglass that, when you turn it over, suddenly you start feeling the pressure to act.
This is your key that helps you lock the door of inertia.
And you must lock it well because it will find many ways to escape in order to create confusion and mislead you.
If, then, you are able to believe and see life as a big game, then it is very likely that you will discover your purpose.
You will realize that you are the protagonist of this game and you will start searching for the rules you must follow and all your possible opponents.
Then you will discover a very big gap.
There are no rules you need to follow, and usually, there are no opponents who want to beat you.
You are, your actions and time.
What exists is your time and your actions.
Even time is a human invention, something you will never be able to understand, and when you realize this, you will understand that you are struggling completely alone.
In decisions of secondary importance, it is useful to examine the pros and cons.
In critical matters, however, decisions must come from your heart.
Therefore, listen and feel your heart, and let it show you the right strategy for your game.
Attach it to a goal and chase it.
If not, you will have enjoyed the journey.
In any case, you will have discovered why it's worth taking a risk, why it's worth striving, and why it's worth playing.
Those who have a "why" to live, can bear with any "how," as Nietzsche said.
This is why we are all called to answer it.
The day you discover it will be the most important day of your life.
Search within yourself without fear and you will find it.
I found it a few years ago after a simple competition.
In order for our economy to get back on a path of stable growth, half a million to a million people must change jobs.
That is, 10 to 20% of the workforce.
And when I say they should change jobs, I don't mean that they should work more productively or more, or more honestly, or more smartly.
If we divide the economy, its sectors into two categories: on the left, the non-tradable ones, that is, those that cannot be provided from afar and thus are not subject to international competition.
And on the right are the tradables, that is, the products that can be transported, the industrial or agricultural goods, and the services that can be offered to foreigners.
Tourism, shipping, and various forms of marketable technology such as software.
The problem with our economy, the structural one, apart from fiscal issues, is that we have very few right-wing jobs and comparatively many left-wing ones.
And in order for us to walk upright, hundreds of thousands of jobs must come from there, here.
Let us divide them into branches that require large and organized enterprises, which means capital, hierarchies, etc., and those that can and are today being done by small businesses.

Those who talk about investments and hope that jobs will come with "investments" in quotation marks, in reality hope that the jobs will come in ready-made packages, here, on top of the big competitive companies, well-designed, located, with their hierarchies and their equipment.
And indeed, this is the international model of an outward-looking economy.
Large industries, large companies in competitive sectors are those that do the exports or those that prevent a large influx of imports, defending the domestic market.
And the reason is that our entire society, our entire institutional environment, colludes to prevent us from having many large enterprises in competitive sectors.
At this moment, less than 5% of the workforce is employed in large companies up there.
And when I say large, I mean having more than 250 employees, not huge.
About 25% work in large organizations or in the state, and mainly in the state, in the non-commercial sectors.
And 70% is the small and medium-sized economy.
And of this 70%, at least half are self-employed and farmers.

Therefore, the major adjustment we have to make is from the entire non-tradable sector and from the whole pool of unemployed to create jobs here, in the small, export-oriented, outward-looking economy.
Now, this is a trajectory that is not at all common internationally.
We must create our own path, quite peculiar, quite Greek.
Greek households will make it because they have certain elements that are not very widespread in the West.
First of all, the self-employed person and small business owner has business acumen.
He knows how to build new ventures, which a senior executive from a big Western company who has built his career within a hierarchy does not necessarily know.
Then there is the famous multitasking of the Greek family.

Different income sources, some various assets, a small plot of land here, some rent from there, a pension, the wife's public sector salary, which is stable, etc. And this is important because; because the international research, the statistical research, on what are the main factors that convince someone to become an entrepreneur, to start their own business, the most significant factor statistically is having a safety net, some small assets, something.
That is, if we have a risk-taking spirit, etc. Therefore, I believe that Greek households have all the elements needed to achieve this major shift I spoke about.

And so I don't think it's hard to imagine a philologist leaving the institute and going into tourism, or an engineer returning to his family's farmland and trying something new, a new crop—there hasn't been much innovation in olive groves, as far as I can see—or three young technicians designing digital applications for the global economy, some of whom are here today.
And one of the biggest is the standards we carry in our heads.
The standards we carry in our heads will be one of the biggest obstacles, standards for what? For example, what is good work.
Or what kind of businesses are the best businesses for the national economy.
In 1980, the dominant, the Western model, was that development means industry.
And we happen to have our models... ...we don't work them out rationally.
There are dominant Western narratives that fit the West but may not fit us.
Well, in the '80s the dominant model said: "development means industry."
And that is why Andreas Papandreou, the charismatic Andreas Papandreou, said at the time, if you remember: "We will not become the waiters of Europe."
And it negatively affected tourism for many years.
Imagine if he had said: "We want to be the hosts of all of Europe."
Come here, meet each other, have fun, be entertained, seek.
How different the narrative for tourism would be.
Today, a dominant pattern says that development is infrastructure and organization.
But for us, we must have a wise balance between infrastructure and organization on one side, and our own special characteristics.
And to continue with an example, tourism.
Foreigners who want to come to one of our islands will land at Eleftherios Venizelos Airport.
They will come into contact with an organized airport, which only large organizations and infrastructure and plans can have.
The top part of the board that I showed before.
But obviously they are not coming for the airport.
Then, they will board a ship, which, if it is fast and clean, will allow them to reach their destination relaxed and refreshed.
And obviously, only a big company can properly operate the ship.
But again, that's not the reason they come on the boat.
The reason they come is, after the boat leaves them on the island, what will they do there? They will go to the small business, rent a donkey, and start their adventures.
And in the afternoon they will go and buy honey that has been produced by another small business at their facilities, which are these.
Well, the airport and the ship are not our products; they are the channels through which people reach our products.
Which are the route with the donkey, the beach, the tavern, etc. And these are offered by the small ones.
And to explain myself, I'm not saying that small "Rooms to Let" are better than large hotel units.
What I am saying is that, since we either do not want or cannot have many large units, the success of tourism will depend on the small professionals.
How hospitable they are, how resourceful they are, how much they take care of the environment, and how much they persuade the customer to pay without feeling that he is being cheated.
I consider, therefore, that the airport and the pigsty are a good metaphor for our entire development model.
We need the good infrastructure that only planning, large hierarchies, and big capital can provide, and in this we must not differ from the West.

But what will define our position in the global economy, which will not be the same as that of the Germans or the Chinese, will be the small entrepreneurs who must find ways to sell their products everywhere.
Where can we rely on? Apart from agriculture or tourism, where we naturally have advantages? There is a cliché that says: "On our youth, their intelligence, and their studies."
Because they are open-minded, they travel, they know languages, and in fact, many of them study at the best universities in the world.
That is, based on these elements, we can have some successes.
Where we can have more is if we combine these elements, wisdom and studies, with some local peculiarities, which I must tell you no one can tell you from the comfort of their seat.
These are discovered by entrepreneurs every day, small entrepreneurs from job to job.
The so-called foreign language books, and especially those in the English language.
I know at least 3 Greek publishers who are very successful internationally.
One of them was bought last year by a multinational company, and the other two make significant exports.
From where to where, then, Greek publishers in the learning of the English language.
In most countries, children learn English at school, and their books are either provided by big multinational companies or by one or two local publishers with long-term contracts.
We here, don't learn English at school.
We learn from the tutoring centers, of which there are six thousand in Greece, and each one decides which books to take and redecides them the following year.
So, a very competitive market of publishers for English language learning has been created, which is about 15 at the moment, and most of them are local, they are Greeks.
Another characteristic of Greece, again a peculiarity, is that Greek students want to finish their English studies, with private tutoring and exams, by the age of 16.
Because after that, the other tutoring classes for the national exams must begin.
This then gives rise to a need for teaching methods that are more concise and faster.
Faster than those developed by multinational publishers.
And so, Greek publishers, stepping on this specificity, have created products that are internationally competitive.
Touching on an institutional peculiarity of Greece.

How many would admit that this form of tutoring, which some dismissively call cramming, has created an expertise that is internationally competitive, something the official school system would never create?
These are the dominant narratives, the dominant patterns that I was talking about before, which sometimes hinder us.
And to give another example; there is a Greek business model, the Internet cafes, where students go in the afternoons and play online adventure games with their friends.

And it is very useful for the students because it develops a spirit of cooperation since they play together, and of course other complex skills, things that the Greek school naturally does not provide.
However, the state approaches Internet cafes with great suspicion, fearing that gambling might be taking place behind the scenes.
And it is trying in various ways to close them.
And entrepreneurs cannot defend their work by citing European standards because they are not comparable.
That is, their particular characteristic, which could be an advantage, turns into a disadvantage due to the state's suspicion.
In 1976, in my first job, I worked on a research program for the ministry of education and the World Bank.
And various foreign consultants would come to help us with the work.
At some point, a professor of economic history from a very large, well-known American university appeared.
We were then in a building on Ermou Street.
During a break at work, he asked me about my thesis—I was then working on a thesis—on the subject of monopolistic concentration in the Greek industry.
I was very influenced at that time by the neo-Marxist theories of development.
The neo-Marxist theories of development, which spoke about the dependence and underdevelopment of the European periphery.
So when he asked me, I started telling him about the capitalist metropolises that concentrate all the technology there, and how underdeveloped my country is.
He listened to me very patiently, and in the end he said, "All of this is fine, but have you noticed the barista's record?" and he meant... this.

He explained to me how wonderfully designed it is, so that it rests comfortably on the fingers and the weight of the glass stabilizes it, and when the coffeehouse waiter goes up and down the ministry stairs, the water doesn't spill.
And he said to me: "So well-designed a disc, I haven't seen in America."
I was telling him about the great stories, and he was telling me about the barista's disk.
That you don't learn real economics by reading, you learn it by observing.
And that technology is not only what exists in Intel's laboratories, but the choices that every professional makes every day.
You might ask me why I say these things; obviously, I don't say them to imply that the discs will save our economy.
I'm saying this because I think Nikos Anagnostou, the blogger who writes about technology, is right when he recently wrote in a very nice post: "Outward orientation doesn't need to be impressive."
Well, our new outward-looking small businesses won't fit into some grand narrative, they won't have the official blessing of the state that, for example, a pharmacy has today.

They will not be considered as a function, nor will they have the security of the public sector, nor the shine of a career in a big company, nor will they have successfully copied models from Business Week.
They will be improvised, they will seem rough and Balkan and mysterious.
Owners alone must invent the narrative that will give meaning and longevity to what they do.
We others shouldn't underestimate them because they are the ones who will save us.
When I was asked to speak at this year's TEDxAcademy, the first obvious question, of course, was what the topic was, and I admit the answer troubled me.
What do I have to do with developments? I can tell you about many unpleasant developments in my country, but that is not the issue.
I thought about it a little, I didn't want to say no.
It was a prestigious invitation, and suddenly I got what the younger people call a "flash."
And I said that here in Europe, for 60 or more years, the most impressive, revolutionary political experiment has been carried out.
I want to tell you the good news, I want to tell you the bad news, and I want to close with a few words about Greece.
Well, my topic today is the European experiment.
What were the goals of this experiment that began in the 1950s? First and foremost, the establishment of peace in a Europe with open borders.
Remember a small continent, fragmented, with many states carrying on their backs a long, glorious history, yet filled with wars and bloody conflicts.

Second goal: achieving economic prosperity through the creation of a large, free, competitive European market, but at the same time by creating the tools that would help the less developed countries and regions of Europe to converge.
Third goal: strengthening democratic institutions, especially in countries with a problematic political past.

Fourth goal: the application of the ancient saying, "strength in unity," that is, in a world that is changing very rapidly, in a world where size matters, European countries, even those once great and strong, no longer have either the weight or the size to be protagonists on the global stage, not even usually secondary players.
What means do we use to achieve these goals? First, establishing a culture of cooperation and dialogue.
In Europe, we elect and negotiate until death.
But, to tell you the truth, I prefer seventeen-hour negotiations, even if they go past midnight, to shooting at each other, as we used to do for centuries in Europe.
Second means: the creation of common institutions and policies, upon which this culture of cooperation will be based.
You might say all these sound nice in words, but in practice, what can actually be done? My answer is simple: a lot has already been done.
Europe became unrecognizable within 50 years.

And do you want me to put it differently? What is the proof of the success of the European political experiment, at least until recent years? Think of Europe as a small shop, which started in the 1950s, small and poor, selling only two products, basically coal and steel.
These were the two products with which the European Coal and Steel Community began.
Today, this small shop has turned into a huge supermarket that sells everything, although always of different quality products, and has greatly increased its customer base.
We went from six members to 28, and many more are crowding at the entrance wanting to get in.
I don't think there is a clearer sign of a store's success than continuously increasing your revenue and customer base.
And I should add something else: until quite recently, you were considered a model for the rest of the world.
You will say that Europe had many crises in the past.
My answer, however, would be that this crisis is fundamental, and it risks becoming an existential crisis for the European political experiment.
Why then crisis? Let me give some explanations.
One reason Europe is in crisis is because sometimes, possibly, we moved too quickly, without sufficient preparation, without considering the consequences of our actions.
We decided in Europe, at the beginning of the 90s, to create a common currency.
This is the peak of the European political experiment.
It is the most advanced stage of political evolution.

But, we created this common currency, but we did not create either the institutional or the political background on which it would be based, nor the economic tools that would make the euro sustainable.
In other words, we tried to repeat in politics and economy the miracle of the immaculate conception.
A currency without a state does not exist in history, and this is something we are learning "the hard way," as the Turks also say.
The second is that we were unprepared, but we were also unlucky.
Why didn't the euro manage to complete ten years of life, and instead came the bursting of the biggest international financial bubble since 1929.
No one could have predicted it, this is an accident.
So it is both a lack of preparation and, obviously, bad luck.
The third category refers to reasons related to the internal affairs of our countries.
What has been happening over the past 20-30 years in European countries, and indeed in all the developed countries of the Western world? Economic inequalities continue to widen.
This is much more intense in the US and the UK than in most countries of continental Europe.

At the same time, the number of our fellow citizens who feel they are losing out, who can no longer cope with the resulting competition, who are losing out from globalization and European integration, is constantly increasing.
These people are turning against globalization and against Europe.
If a way is not found to address this problem, the problem will further escalate in the coming years.
Fourth reason is: it's not the best neighborhood.
Let me remind you, if you look at the map of Europe, we have only neighboring countries in the east and south.
In the north there are only polar bears and in the west we have fish.
So, we are dealing with neighbors in the East and the South.
The majority of Europe's neighbors belong to the category of the losers of globalization.
And in all this economic problem, in recent years, war and religious conflicts have been added.
One of the results of all these problems in our neighborhood is the huge increase in migration flows.
The migration crisis is at risk of becoming in Europe a crisis before which the euro crisis would pale, and a crisis that could blow Europe apart.
Europe certainly needs to show solidarity with people who are being persecuted.
It needs to discuss a fairer distribution of the burdens.
And this is the effective control of Europe's external borders, in general, and of Greece, since it is part of the whole of Europe.
Thus, due to this crisis in recent years, we have reached a situation in which our coexistence within the EU is becoming difficult.
Dissatisfaction is growing, and acceptance of the European political experiment is continuously decreasing.

And for this reason, this sometimes resembles a symbiosis, which I called in a book I wrote last year and which has been published in various languages, "the unhappy union," that is, Europe is at risk of becoming a symbiosis in a polygamous relationship in which the partners remain together, not so much because they love each other, but because they fear the cost of divorce, or because they fear being left alone.
You will tell me that fear keeps many marriages together.
But I don't think it is enough for a successful European coexistence.
As you know, Greece has been an integral part of this European political experiment for many decades, I hope.

I would say that the people in Greece, our fellow citizens, at least those who consider our participation in the European political experiment as a positive development, can be divided into three major categories.
The first category includes our fellow citizens who believe that Europe is like a cow you milk as much as you possibly can.
These people usually have a very relaxed approach to how common rules are applied.
Any rules, whether Greek or European.

A second category views Europe as the secure anchor for a ship, the Greek one, which often appears rudderless, because the crew members constantly quarrel with each other, because they do not let the captain take the helm, because each one has a different opinion about where they should go.
Thus, in this category, Greece offers a safe anchor.
There is also a third category, to which, I think, fewer people belong.

These are the ones who from the beginning considered our participation in the European political experiment as an additional, not the only, means to achieve a goal that goes back a long way, to the Greek Revolution, if you will, or even before: the creation of a modern, European state in Greece, a state of law with strong democratic institutions, with economic prosperity and social solidarity.
Obviously, these three categories have always been the overwhelming majority of Greek men and women.
Others, for whom Europe either says nothing or has never meant anything, perhaps because Europe represents what they themselves call "urban values" and "urban democracy," which do not satisfy them.
Either because some others imagine a Greece in another part of the world that is not exactly clear where that would be.
And of course, in recent years, with the big crisis, the extreme nationalists have also emerged from their caves.
Thus, this crisis in Europe is changing the internal balances within the country.
Greece, unfortunately, has ended up being the most problematic member, certainly of the Eurozone, most likely of the entire European Union, in recent years.
And of course, the big question is, "Who is to blame for all of this?" I don't have the time, I still have about three minutes left, to answer this question for you.

I believe that the management of the Greek crisis in recent years will be, is already being, but will even more so in the future, a lesson that will be taught in an increasing number of universities, not only in Greece, but internationally.
Because it is exactly an example of a crisis that tells you what not to do in a similar crisis.
And the responsibilities concern not only, to be fair, the country's political leadership and our society in general, but also concern our European partners and the International Monetary Fund.
It is, in other words, an awareness of a great, enormous responsibility for a crisis management that has failed until now, the cost of which is obviously very high.
What do we do from here on? Certainly, Europe must change if it wants to overcome the crisis.
I know that at TEDx, the speaker who comes here is expected to deliver a positive message.
If I brought you only a positive message, I think I would be deceiving you.
What is at stake today in Europe is immense.
Europe will certainly overcome this crisis as well, more united and changed.
But the risk of Europe collapsing or even having splits or partial dissolution is really visible.

And the first thing I wanted to say to all of you is that you should think of the success of the European political experiment as being everyone's responsibility, as something that directly concerns all of us and our children.
If the European political experiment fails, one of its greatest victims will be this country.
So, then, do your best, as active citizens of the European Union, to contribute, as much as possible, each one of you, to the success of this European experiment.
But I believe that before Europe changes, we must change Greece.
We must, finally, be honest, look into the mirror, take responsibility, see what is going wrong in this country, and try to free the country from its bonds.
Only when we start changing Greece will we have gained a credible and serious voice on how Europe will change.
They come to us through questions, images, coincidences, and even dreams.
Ideas excite us, they lead us to wonderful roads, which are full of challenges and full of unexpected developments.
They give us power, but they also have their own dynamics.
A dynamic that helps them evolve and influence more and more people, who in turn feed them back, thus keeping the enthusiasm and inspiration they exude undiminished.

Although I prepared a speech and rehearsed it, I hope it's good enough to present to you today. I'm sure the ideas and associations that come up will likely deviate from what I wanted to say, but let me start and we'll see.
The German philosopher Edmund Husserl had described that each person is the center of the world.
And this is because it perceives the world with itself as a starting point and as a standard of comparison.
What he described as the "nul point," as he named it, is that every person has a completely personal image of the world, which passes through their emotional or logical filter.
We Greeks could say: between his Apollonian and Dionysian self.
This balance between our two sides has gradually begun to decline.
Although we perceive the world mainly through experience - that is, we describe a distance not in meters, but in how many minutes it takes us to walk it - despite this, we try to quantify everything.

The ideas that have brought me here today concern architecture, but particularly an architecture that attempts to satisfy the conditions of the modern and future human being, through the bridging of emotion and logic, of the experiential and the metric space.
We understand this from the intense pace of life and often from the frequent changes in our way of life.
Conventional architectural rigidity does not seem to satisfy this condition.
And for this reason, the first idea, which I will talk to you about, is flexibility.
Flexibility is not something new - and personally, I have often applied it within an architectural framework, whether as research or as practice.
But I realized its true dimension from another occupation.
As a set designer and assistant director in the amateur theater I belonged to.

There, mainly due to [incomprehensible], but also due to the challenge of creating the appropriate environment with the greatest impact at the lowest cost, I often built the sets using multi-functional objects.
That is, objects which, depending on how they were placed in space and their orientation, created a completely different feeling.
The feeling that was necessary to convey the feeling the play wanted.

This very dimension, that is, how we understand the environment differently, I considered as a fundamental element of variability, something I searched for and I think I understood sufficiently to gradually lead me to the creation of a theory that optimizes the use of space in time.
In other words, we could use the space as best as possible in time, so that we don't waste it.
This was called spatial economy or spatial economics.
But my involvement with theater, the truth is, led me to something else that interested me: the position of the human being in architecture.
The position of the human, theoretically, is at the center of design.
Nevertheless, what I noticed is that it participates more as dimensions.
In directing and stage design, the basis is an action, an activity, and the human being is always at the center of this.
It completes him as an expression, it completes him as a general sense.
That architecture should perhaps be more concerned with this feeling, with this experience, emerging from the activity itself, and not in some other way.
So, it was born—or rather, because this was already in operation—it was activity-based design, that is, design based on activity.
This is a design direction that has a qualitative difference from conventional design.
That is: understanding the activity not as fragmented use, which leads to ergonomics, but as a whole.
As something that has a temporal imprint and varies experientially according to time.
This thing, this dimension, this perspective, means that we enrich the architectural question beyond ergonomics.
With other features, so that the architectural response is better.
Here I would like to emphasize the following: The role of architecture is to provide answers.
The answers depend on how well the questions were phrased.
And in our case: a better architectural solution.

Thus, the creation of a methodology, which was enriched with more issues for which space should be addressed, led to the spatial needs around an activity being better defined, so that architecture could come to clothe them.

Thus, the logic of the dynamic building program was created, which in turn led to the creation of an online platform, where people, through an application they are likely already using, such as Google Calendar, can enter their activities and thus better understand their spatial needs or what kind of spaces they need in order to perform these activities better.
And in this way, first of all, to evaluate the existing spaces they have, but also to better identify future ones based on their future needs.
Of course, this application could not be implemented without widespread information.
I belong to the generation that lived through the entire transition to today's digital culture, let's say.
I was 13 years old when the first personal computers came out, and I was perhaps one of the first people to get involved with them.
Well, it was an activity that was kept secret, as it wasn't very popular back then because we had the girls.
And I experienced the whole process of how gradually everyone started getting involved.
Indeed, to such an extent that with some old friends of mine we used to discuss that we probably should stop dealing with it because this mass acceptance is strange.
The issue is that these two, that is, this digital age brought two, let's say, basic concepts into our lives.
This one of automation and this one of connectivity.
[automation + interconnection] Automation is something that is done for us without our involvement.

In other words, it's something that is considered beneficial, programmed to happen in this way without us spending time and energy. Connectivity, on the other hand, is the ability, in various ways, to communicate and connect with distant people and places.
These two are so intense today, so widespread, so given, that their absence is actually unpleasant for us.
This means that these two technologies are perfectly logical to be integrated into the space.
In other words: it is a logical next step, and this leads to robotic architecture.
Robotic architecture—the research I have conducted and my engagement with it—has clearly and certainly shown me that it is applicable.
That is, the technical problems I thought about, in the application they seemed to be solved and they are really solved.
It is essentially the epitome of variability in architecture.

Beautiful? It is really a combination of many ideas with different origins that came and completed a puzzle, a riddle, which I considered to be complete, that is, from the perspective of theory, methodology, or application, all the pieces had been put in place so that the robotic architecture could be applied.
So I considered that a greater idea had been formed and completed.
I was so excited about it that I even wrote a book about it.
The point is, however, as I told you before, that ideas are alive.
And this came to prove it to me, something simple, a door.
You'll say, what did a door do? It opened.
You'll tell me, what did you expect from a door? Come on!
The issue is that this door opened while I was working and I was absorbed, and I was disturbed, quite disturbed.
Indeed, to the extent that this started to concern me because it happened, and suddenly I think I had one of the most important ideas I've had in this field.
In other words, even if something is considered useful, expected, or even if we ourselves have asked for it to happen, if it happens at the wrong time, it is unpleasant.
[the strange thing] And this, suddenly, rang that bell for me in something that, in a way, was lurking beneath, but I couldn't see it.
In other words, while robotic architecture seems to have so many benefits, why isn't it more widespread? And I understood why it can be annoying.
What makes communication between people unobtrusive and pleasant is the quality of empathy and understanding.
That is, the ability to understand, to read a situation, and to adjust our behavior so that it is compatible with that situation.
If someone lacks this ability, they behave exactly the same or similarly in all possible situations, which creates an awkwardness that in turn leads to a reduction in communicativeness.
Unfortunately, robotic systems have a predictable and usually similar behavior.

And this can be excellent if we have a repetitive task, such as assembling something or screwing a screw 24 hours a day, but when you are dealing with people, this is problematic, because people often change their mood and behavior.
And in order to be pleasant, you should follow it.

The integration of sensitivity and understanding into the cognitive characteristics of a robotic system essentially gave birth to what I might call a design methodology or technology integration approach in architecture, called "Sensponsive," derived from the word "sensibility," meaning sensitivity with understanding, and "responsive," meaning response.
Such a system, therefore, will never react to a situation unless it first understands the people's intentions in the space.
Of course, a system that would fully understand all situations is far beyond our capabilities.
What we can do and are working on is, depending on the activity we want to benefit through our architecture, studying the dominant factors.
That is, those factors that benefit it more directly.
Another feature of this application, beyond the ability to understand humans, is sensitivity, that is, what we said before, that the behavior must be such that it is appropriate to the circumstances.
This means that the system is trying to behave appropriately, in order to be as non-intrusive as possible.
To minimize this dimension and to be as useful as possible.
Now you might ask, where do we apply it? And how? At the moment, we have two main directions in which we are working on this theory.
One relates to the enhancement of learning.
The goal is to create an original lecture space, where the audience can maintain their attention for a longer time and be able to learn more from the lecture.
We achieve this by monitoring two critical parameters: one is attention and the other is alertness.

When the level of these two factors drops enough for us to realize that the audience is likely getting the least from this lecture, a system of subtle interventions in the space is activated. We have experimentally confirmed in the lab that when these interventions occur, they activate the audience and revive their attention.
Thus, with this Sensponsive intervention, we enable the audience of a lecture to receive whatever the lecture has to offer most, and the only thing left is the lecture itself to give it.
The other direction has to do with something mixed.
It has to do with the concept of habitation and the concept of psychological balance.
The goal of this research was to find out how we can counteract the negative effects of mild stress and depression, often caused by the intense pace of life and work in the modern world.

For various reasons, this research eventually focused more on extreme environments, and thus we ended up studying and building functional prototypes of a habitat module for astronauts on the International Space Station.

The purpose of this system was to understand people's moods inside, so that it could change the regulation and environmental conditions, in order to help them relax better, find psychological balance, rest more, and endure the continuation of their mission.
Indeed, this research opened another door for us.

We were called to NASA, in Houston, and more specifically to the Human Research Program, as this is one of the few approaches that actually proposes a solution for improving the living conditions of humans on long-duration space missions.
Something, that is designed for the near future.
I think that all these ideas and especially the application frameworks they led to, really describe a window into the future.
They describe the application of an interactive architecture aimed at creating truly beneficial environments free from negative effects.
In general, I believe this is what the vision of technology should be.
Hope for a tomorrow that we believe will be better.
Personally, in order to keep this perspective clear, I write science fiction novels.
so that I can mentally experience the worlds I would like to build.
But the basic component for all of these is ideas.
Ideas are alive and must spread.
[Idea] Let your ideas spread, grow, evolve, because only through them can we live in tomorrow's world today.
(Clapping) Today, I would like to talk to you about the value of truth in our daily lives.

First, do we tell truths? Let's start with us here today. Who among you can remember telling a truth, we don't need to say which one, to a friend, a partner, yourself, in the last month, let's say, and raise a hand.
Who has told a truth? Bravo, we tell truths!
I'm glad, because I didn't know what the audience's reaction would be to this question.
But what exactly do we mean when we call something truth? Many have claimed that truth is a reflection of our reality to which we do not always have access, because our senses are imperfect.
Others claim that truth is a personal matter, it is subjective for each of us and changes with the unfolding of our lives.
Today, I would like to refer to truth as defined by Heraclitus, who told us that the true is the opposite of oblivion, that is, something that needs to be revealed and brought into the light.

This truth is connected with many aspects of our daily lives, indeed, it is connected with reality, revealing aspects of it, enhancing our understanding and perception of the world, promoting self-awareness and authenticity in our personal expression, while often leading to honesty and decision-making.
In my personal path, truth has always been connected with architecture and the city.

Now you might ask, what connection can the city have with truth? The theoretical architect Lewis Mumford has told us that the ultimate mission of the city is to intensify the conscious participation of human beings in the cosmic and historical process.

This multiplication of all dimensions of life, through emotional connection, logical communication, technological artistry, and above all, dramatic representation, has been the most important task of the city throughout history.

This saying made me think that our cities function like accelerators, meaning they accelerate processes, relationships, and events in which we exist, with our values and our way of life, that is, cities create chain reactions that increase our awareness.
Mamford tells us that this happens through emotional connection, logical communication, technological craftsmanship, and dramatic representation.
Today, I will focus more on technological craftsmanship and dramatic representation as I have encountered them through my work.
Before we talk about technological excellence, I want to take a step back and look at the tools we use.
According to theoretical architects Beatriz Colomina and Mark Wigley in their book "Are We Human?", they argue that the tools we create ultimately create us.
They claim, starting from Darwin's theory, that it is the human hand that has adapted to its tools, and not the other way around.
In other words, our relationship with technology is reciprocal, and they continue by saying that human creations are surfaces of interaction, which, as we shape them, shape us as well.

Let's take our mobile phone, which you all know, and perhaps you have noticed that the changes in the device's design over the years have also changed the way we behave, our habits, even our gaze and the way we walk. Therefore, a technological device can change us.

How can we reverse this process? How could we influence this relationship? What if we designed our own mobile phones and their applications? What if we designed all of our technological tools? This question, along with many others, was answered by the Maker Movement.
The Maker Movement is a movement that aims to democratize technology through the creation of free software and hardware.

About 15 years ago, in places like hacker spaces, maker spaces, and other online communities, groups of inventors, researchers, and programmers began sharing technological platforms with the general public and creating new technological tools accessible to everyone.

For example, microprocessors were created, such as Arduino, Arduino lilypad, Raspberry Pie, among others, and corresponding programming languages with which we can easily program our own applications and create our own tools.

What has the Maker Movement essentially achieved with this? It has succeeded in bringing new tools into the light, taking them out of the black box of technology that they were for most of us until now.

Another thing the Maker Movement achieved is changing our relationship with technology, making it a game. Through easy experimentation, many of us have come closer to technology to play, experiment, and often create innovative applications out of nowhere.
One final and very important element that the Maker Movement achieved is that it brought us closer together.
That is, through the sharing of knowledge, through our offline and online communities, it brought us together to create, and it taught us to work collectively, in teams.

But what is the value of open technologies in the public sphere, and especially in the field of art that concerns me? The political scientist Sadhal Mouf has told us that art in the public space needs to question the given order of things, the status quo.
When technology is used in art, it needs not to repeat mainstream applications, the mainstream way in which we use it, but rather to be used in a different way, in order to create new associations.
Why? Because we need this plurality of voices in the public space, because it promotes dialogue among us.

Thus, open technologies lead us precisely in this direction, because through accessibility and experimentation they allow a large group of users, both specialists and amateurs, to engage, propose new meanings, and new representations in the public space.

Speaking of representations, I return to Mamphord's quote, who told us that dramatic representation is important, and for the first time I encountered this concept when I was studying architecture here, at the School of Architecture in Patras, many years ago, and I was wondering which direction to follow.

Among the visual arts, urban planning, and landscape architecture, I was a bit undecided. At one point, I did a project that brought to the surface my love for cultural landscapes; that is, I wanted to explore the relationship between the human body and gaze with their immediate natural and artificial environments through small-scale architecture.
Also, at that time I had a curiosity about technology and how technology could contribute to these experiences, so I went to London to do a similar master's degree.
In one of my first works I started to explore these representations and the relationship between natural and artificial.
For example, I created the Wind chime, which is a representation of a metal melody.
I don't know if you know it, it's that musical instrument we put on the veranda, and it makes a metallic melody when the wind blows.
In my case, in my version, I replaced the metal pipes with plastic Wii remote controls, which I programmed to produce metallic sounds, imitate them, and gradually transform them into noise.

Another representation, which I explored through the Fabrique, this work, in which I wanted to explore our relationship with a nature that is not natural, a mechanical nature, and essentially challenge the concept of nature as we know it, to see what lies behind.
This particular work, when one shines light on these pots, causes the chains on the ceiling to start moving, and in the end, they synchronize into a small dance.
At some point, in the course of my artistic search, I decided to follow another path, that of play.
So in 2011, with two collaborators, we created the Athens Plaython.

At that time in Athens, we often had violent incidents through demonstrations in the streets of Athens, which were accompanied by arson and destruction. We wanted to give a different voice, a hopeful voice, through play and togetherness.
And we saw our city changing, we saw many people, perhaps even thousands, especially in the last year, coming to play with us for a weekend, and we saw the city space changing.

How did this happen? Through play—why? Because play has this property, to introduce us in a magical way into an inner world of fantasy. For example, it can transform an urban space into a battlefield, where different groups can negotiate a neighborhood, or gorgons, dragons, knights, and so on can start to appear.
This world of fantasy, however, is not independent of the urban space; on the contrary, it encompasses it.
That is, the urban space is enhanced through play, acquiring new characteristics that are collectively created.
So at that moment I realized that for me, this means of expression, the game, is important because it brings all of us into it and creates a sense of community.

Later, I created my studio, Entropika, together with a team of collaborators, and we continue to create interactive installations in public space. For example, one of our projects, Cloudscapes, creates an exact representation of a natural phenomenon, clouds and rain.
It essentially mimics the sound of thunder and rain, and the light of lightning, when passersby go underneath.
It was another opportunity to explore how these representations affect the urban space.

Indeed, in this particular work, it revealed a truth after many years: while the first works were more theoretical, with ideas I had processed very thoroughly and which I particularly liked, a work like this one, simpler, with a simpler idea but a playful mood, had much greater immediacy with the public. We had children trying to enchant it by shouting "Abracadabra," we had passersby putting on their hoods, believing they would be struck by lightning, and we had a teenager sitting under the cloud, and as soon as he heard thunder, he kept shouting "Thor."
(Laughter) On the same path, we continued with the Playstools, where we again explored the playful dimensions of the objects we were making, and created a hybrid version of the musical chairs game.

In this particular game, these scampi have additional properties; we wouldn't just use natural objects. Each scampi has two touch sensors, they have lights, produce sound, and communicate with each other through a wireless network, essentially because they are convenient and can fit into any space like that.
They invite the players to interact with the tokens in each round of the game, and the team that wins the most in color wins.
Indeed, we want the future of this game to become an open educational platform where children, perhaps wiser designers than us, can upload their own games and change the game's rules as they wish.
At this point, I even put a period last January, because I decided to go to Matera, Italy, where I work at the Open Design School.
The Open Design School is a pilot program of the cultural capital Matera 2019.
There we are asked to design structures for the public space, which will serve all cultural events for this and the next year.
These structures can be stands, information stands, theatrical stages, and so on.

The interesting thing here is that we are again using open design tools, that is, through the philosophy of Open Structures. If you know what that is, if you don't, it is an online platform, a website, and a community where everyone can share their designs freely, without copyright, with the rest of the community, and everyone can further develop them and share them again online.
The second interesting point is that here as well, we are called upon to design through play and experimentation, and this follows accordingly.
In other words, we are not called to design the known constructions, such as stands that look like stands—anyone can do that.
We are called to explore through experimentation and design mysterious, playful objects that raise questions for the residents and the city, without immediately providing answers.

So throughout my journey, I have often returned to Maffort's initial question: what kind of technology do we need, what is the technological excellence, and what representations do we need in the city? The personal answer I have given is that the technological excellence we need must be open and accessible to all.
As for representations, I have seen that many of them need to be participatory, collective, and invite us to play with them.
Of course, this is my answer, it is my truth, and none of us needs to deal with art and technology in order to see the value of truth within the city.
Mamford, at the beginning, if you remember, had said that truth in the city emerges both through emotional connection and logical communication.
Thus, our cities are ourselves, and regardless of each person's status, each of us can genuinely contribute to our collective life.
So today, I would like to invite you to share a truth with your colleague, with your companion, with a friend, but before all else, I would like to invite you to share a truth with yourself.
Since I am of the old school, I take the microphone and not the one they put in the ear.

It's redundant for me to tell you, because I see a lot of young people and I know they've taken the Panhellenic exams, I feel the same way tonight, and I have the same nervousness, as if I have to take the Panhellenic exams myself.
Nevertheless, I prefer being as old as I am rather than being 18 and having to take the national exams in Greece.
(Laughter) (Applause) I will tell you a story, a fairy tale that has many beginnings.
Many people have a starting point in their lives.
I don't know why, but I got a lot of them, and every time I started from a new road.

As far as I remember myself, I remember being on the island of Samos, although I was not born there. I went there when I was three years old with my sister, who was one and a half years older than me, because our mother had tuberculosis and sent us to stay with my grandfather and his older sister.
For me, the whole world was Samos, this was Greece.

The pines, the sea, the grandfather—whom we thought was a Greek because he was a teacher of ancient Greek, he was retired—spoke only to us about ancient times, told us myths, and we felt that this was a happy place that we didn't want to lose at all.
And what we mainly saw is that in life, just as you put on your shoes in the morning, you should also take a book.
Because we always saw our grandfather holding a book in his hand.
When our grandfather left, I thought he took everything with him, and it was a fresh start.

Soon after, we went to Athens, we went to school, and at school I felt the first thing, which is friendship—I became friends with George Sari, and until the last moment, when she left forever, we remained friends.
And this, kids, I tell you to the young ones, is that friendship plays a very big role in life.
At school I started reading, and indeed, with my sister we really loved reading poetry, especially sad poems: by Palamas, "Unshaken and unadorned, I do not give you to the Grave."
Of Polemi... and we would cry, we would read them at night and cry.
And so something went inside me, maybe I should write a poem too.
My mother had a twin brother, a most beautiful person—both outwardly and inwardly.
He was a math teacher and engaged to a girl who didn't like us at all because we considered him - he was also my grandfather - as belonging to us.
They used to call this girl Didō from above, she had a slightly crooked nose, but she had eyes that sparkled with fire.
This Dido became our great writer, Dido Sotiriou.
And many may have known her as an author, but whoever hasn't known her as a person has missed out on a lot.
I wrote her a poem for her wedding.
She smiled, but when I looked into her eyes, they seemed so sad, and I made a decision in my life that I would never become a poet.
(Laughter) Well, I'll tell you children the poem because 82 years have passed, and while I was staying often in Brussels, it came to my mind one evening, about my daughter.
I'll tell you so that you understand what poetry was saved from, what you and I were saved from.

"At dawn with the dew, my dear grandmother, where I went in my little boat for a swim, I saw a white heron flying gracefully. Run and tell your grandmother Chara, tra la la, tra la la." (Laughter) (Applause) Did you think I was lost? That I could become a poet? I think so, yes.
I forgot to tell you that in my grandfather's house in Samos, in the living room, there was a display cabinet, and inside there was a tiger, the "kaplani."
They said that in Samos, and I thought everyone in Greece knew it when I was writing the book "The Kaplani (of the Vitrina)."
Neither Kaplanis knew then, nor I, what role we would both play in our lives.
With Didō, we learned many things; she made us want to love something, to care about something, to read, of course.
War came, and Dido put us in resistance.
And our first act of resistance that we did—Dido used to tell us, "Don't think it's nothing, it's something significant."
As much as my father was away at the bank where he worked in the morning and came back home in the afternoon, our house became a mess.
Dido brought the women of the resistance: Electra, who was executed by the Germans, Melpo Axioti, Zevo, and Svoli.

They used to gather and when they left - because they smoked like chimneys - our act of resistance with my sister was to open the balcony door, air out the room, and get rid of the smell of smoke so that our father wouldn't come home and see such a situation.

We organized ourselves in the EPON, and as we remember now, and many times with Zoso, as long as she was alive, we would sit and say, "Don't say anywhere that the years of the occupation were happy years."
Because we believed in something, because we had a vision, because we believed that we really helped free Greece.
And we had a vision that we said, when the occupation ends, we will live out this vision of ours.

Now, if it didn't come out, it's something else... During the occupation, I went to the Aedonopoulou school, and we had a teacher in the arts who wanted to take us out of this sorrow, this gloom, and hunger, and make our minds think of other things. She said to us, "Children..."—she had studied in France at the fine arts school—"...we will do a puppet theater."
He says to me, "What do you know how to do?" I say, "Nothing."
He asked me my name. "A," he says, "are you Alki Zee?" And I say, "Yes."
Because a small piece of mine had been published in the school magazine.

Alki Zee: Will you hold it for me?  
DCG: Yes.
(Laughter) (Applause) He told me, "Then write plays for the puppet theater."

And I simply sat down and wrote plays for the puppet theater, which we performed, and one day she told us, "Now look, be very good because some friends will come," because they were the children from the school who were watching.
She was very close to Empirikos and brought us, more or less, Empirikos, Elytis, and Mario Ploritis.
Whose Mario Ploritis brought a friend of his, who together with Koun, was one of the founders of the Koun Theatre, called George Sebastikoglou, and later became my companion.
And my sister for a while kept saying to me, "Who are you going out with, again with that one with the ugly surname?"
We simply loved what we were doing.
And when those we had, the great people, the poets—who were very simple and treated us very kindly—invited us to have coffee at Lumidis, and from then on we often went there and listened.
And there were other girls, I don't know why girls and not boys, they didn't go.
And we listened to them, you know, with great joy and learned things.
That is, we learned more from them than from the university.
At EPON, we had all-night parties because we couldn't go home late, as movement was prohibited.
And we would set up a phonograph, we would pretend to dance so that we could print illegal newspapers, press, proclamations and others.
Of course, in the meantime we were already dancing, already flirting.
At the piano, a child with a long scarf played all night, and we put black raisins in his mouth so he wouldn't be hungry all night.
(Clapping) October 12th is a day that is neither celebrated in Greece, nor is it taught in school, it was the day of liberation.
As far as I remember, it was the happiest day of my life.
Where we were set free, where we believed that all our dreams would come true.
But it didn't last many days, and December of '44 began, which I wish had never existed, neither in history nor in my life.
That's when I understood what civil war means.
What does it mean to split into two?
What would my friend next to me at the desk I had say, when we arrived on foot and were going to Volos, and I knocked on her door—she looked at me as if I were her greatest enemy.
And there I said, it's no longer possible for us not to love each other, the people we were close to.
And all that wildness that December had, I haven't forgotten.

And now, when I see the refugees walking kilometers to reach safety, I remember how we used to walk to escape after December ended. Back then, there were no sleeping bags or anything; we put newspapers and slept hidden, and they were shooting at us from above.
I have never been as afraid in my life as I was in December of '44.
There was some relief, things started to become smooth in Greece.
In the meantime, I married Giorgos Sebastikoglou and I also went to the Drama School.
I forgot to mention that I was writing short stories and publishing them in a magazine called "Youth Voice."

And since George Sebastikoglou was very close to Koun, I would go to Koun's theater, and this is very important; during the black occupation, Koun taught the people Ibsen, American theater, real theater.
Despite the hunger they had, the actors and he himself were hungry, but they created a memorable theater, and its roots remain today.
My George was saying, "I think you write, you write."
I didn't go to the school where he taught, to Rota's school, because it was certain he wouldn't accept me.
I went to the Athens Conservatory school, where Rontiris and Veakis taught, and they said that Rontiris made a spoon talk.
Meanwhile, a civil war is slowly beginning again.
George is called up for the army and he leaves Greece secretly.
And they sent me for a walk in Chios too.
That's where I met the women of Greece, because I had never gone outside of Athens.
I've met women from Mytilene, from Northern Greece, from islands, from everywhere.

Women who came not for ideology, who had no idea, but had mainly lost their children, many from Mytilene, who were partisans, and they brought them there, and when they were told that they were free, they cried because they realized that their son had been killed.
I apprenticed there for a year and then I came back.
I didn't know where George was for almost two years.
Until through the Milieux, who was then in Paris, I got a letter telling me to try to leave Greece and go find him.
They had gone after the civil war to Tashkent, and since I'm uncharted, my children say that if I knew where Tashkent is, they wouldn't have been born.
(Laughter) I finally managed, with much effort and great means, to obtain a passport and left.
And I went to Italy, waiting for the Soviet visa, which took two years to arrive, and I reached Moscow.
And when I got off the train and saw that Yiorgos wasn't waiting for me, I panicked.
I say, "Where am I going, where are they sending me?"
In the end, I received them there, I didn't know any Russian or anything, a little French, a little Italian, I could manage to communicate.
They put me on a train, and there were some strange women in costumes, dressed up, weird people with beards.
And I traveled five nights and four days.
And I was passing through Stepa, and every now and then I would ask, "Tashkent?" I thought maybe the train would pass and I would go somewhere else.
And I learned something that immediately upset me: that they had not allowed him to come.
Not the Soviets, our own, the Greek party.
They had not allowed him to come to pick me up in Moscow.
New start, this time a bit difficult because I had to get used to where I am, who I am, what I do.
I haven't written anything there, I don't write anything, I'm trying to learn the language.
My daughter is born, and, fortunately, we are going to Moscow.
We were completely isolated from Greece, while in Moscow we could even call, and the ice began to break, and through the Greek-Soviet connection intellectuals and artists started to arrive.
And one of the first people to arrive was Empirikos, whom I hadn't seen for 20 years.

And I went to the train to meet him, and the performance that the Empiricist had seen—because I was writing parodies, the "klapsodies" I called them, variations on the stories of Odysseus—and the work he had seen was "Kalypso," who was in love with Odysseus and was a surrealist woman with a vivid imagination.
We had just learned about Surrealism then.
And as Odysseus leaves, she commits suicide and says, "I am a withered reed."
(Laughter) When the train arrived, the first one to get off was Empirikos, and as soon as he saw me, with my twenty years, he said, "There goes the sad little sapling."
Why... (Applause) I didn't think I was doing anything special and I didn't even put in a carbon copy, at least, to have a copy.
I left, they put it in a basement, the mice ate it, and nothing is left.
I was writing short stories and sending them to the arts review.

And then I wanted, because I had given birth and my son was born in the meantime - Tarkovsky babysat him, because he was a student and wanted to earn a little money, and through a friend who knew him, he did the babysitting.
(Appause) Perhaps this is why he became a director, and from his father of course, but he wanted for the cinema.
I wanted my children to learn about Greece.
And what more real stories could I tell you than my childhood with my sister on the island of Samos?
And there was born "The Vitrina's Kaplani."
I sent it to Greece, it was a publishing house, Themelio.
And in a little while, in a year I get a leave to go with my children for two months to Greece.
He was a friend, a very valuable person, Dimitris Despotsidis.
I tell him, "What happened to that Kaplanis?" He says, "Look, it's in the showcase."
He had published it and had put it on display.
The Vitrina Backpack, I admit, if it weren't for the teachers, it wouldn't have been circulated, it wouldn't have entered the schools.
Why so many people fought to keep this book out of the schools and to keep me out of them, of course.
But the teachers insisted on introducing it in schools, and now it's been fifty years since Kaplanis is in the schools.
(Clapping) We came back to Greece, I was ten years old, my husband was fifteen.
Come on, let our children learn the language again.
They were speaking, of course, but let's get used to Greece.
Two years later the dictatorship was established and we left for Paris.
The two years in Greece, I didn't write anything.
In France I started writing most of my books: "Peter's Walk," "The Purple Umbrella," and even "Achilles' Engagement"—I started writing that one there as well.

After half an hour, I park my car in an area of Athens.
I empty my pockets of various small objects, I keep only a book, my ID, and I turn off my mobile phone.
I press a button on this gate, and like magic, this iron door opens.
I am now in a space that is full of detectors.

In an inspection, you know, it's like the airport security check when we're traveling to another country, to an exotic island. If I've forgotten something in my pockets, I'll have to go through the inspection again.
I hand over my ID and my mobile phone, they keep them, and give me a card with a specific color and number.
This card, from now on, will be my passport to this strange island.

Holding the card in my hands, I feel that now I must overcome my own personal limits.
It's like I'm standing on a cliff, and I have to jump into the void.
The first door opens, and then closes tightly behind me on its own.
Now, in succession, I hear metal doors closing and opening, I pass through detectors, I've lost count, I don't know how many there are.
And slowly, something unprecedented begins to affect me.
Now I find myself in front of a final door, which I must open alone, and I must enter into a room.
Inside this room, 20 to 25 inmates are waiting for me.
I've seen many movies with prisons, I've seen people who stare at you with strange, hard looks, criminals, with whom you wouldn't have anything to say, you would be afraid even to sit next to them.
All these imaginary people start swirling around in my head like smoke, pushing into my thoughts and threatening me.
I take a deep breath, open the door, and enter.
A warm, smiling face comes toward me, a warm handshake disarms me.
He tells me his first name, then twenty men come toward me, they also tell me their first names, greet me warmly, and ask if I want tea or coffee.
And then I see faces, thirsty, moved, aching.
All these wonderful people I had met outside this door are slowly starting to disappear from my mind.

I cannot speak about today without explaining a bit about my yesterday.
My story ultimately begins when I was eight years old.
I live with my grandfather and grandmother, my parents live abroad, and I live in a loving, caring, and protective environment.
The scene changes when I'm eight years old; I go abroad—to France, to Paris—and I go to an internal school, I don't speak the language, I speak it very little, so I don't understand.
I don't have friends, I don't know my classmates, I don't know the school, I don't know the country, and most importantly, I am far away from my family.
From absolute protection, I move to absolute emptiness.
I feel incredible loneliness, I feel sadness, I feel cut off, I feel excluded, I feel invisible, I feel forgotten.
Yet deep inside me, I want to get out of this situation.
But since I am small, I don't know how.
I instinctively try, through emotion, to find some tricks to escape from my own daily routine.
I create within my mind an emotional microscope, into which I enter and simultaneously become both spectator and protagonist.

Whatever is said to me, whatever I say, whatever I do, whatever is done to me, it goes through this emotional microscope, and so gradually I try—to translate? To decode? To convey whatever I feel in order to understand the what, how, where, when, why, of my own story.
In this process, a very big role was played for me by the "it doesn't matter"s.
The only thing I can do is bring him close to me, try to manage him, get used to him.
If I fall, I will get up again, and the more times I fall, the more I will learn to fall on my feet and to get up again more quickly.
With all these, I gradually managed to walk along with my feelings and mainly to accept them and accept myself.
So over time, gradually, from the "it doesn't matter," I moved to very strong "I want," "I don't want."
My want and don't want is that I don't want a borrowed life, I don't want a life with a discount, I don't want to live in parentheses; I want my own life, the way I believe it should be lived.
I want people not to feel what I felt.
And if it happens and he feels it, I would like to try to do something about it.
So life brought me close to a part of the population, which is also invisible, forgotten, outside the world, which I had not seen.

You are afraid that you might say something you shouldn't or do something you shouldn't.
I don't even want it to enter my imagination that this could happen to me.
After all these thoughts, I came face to face with many stories, I lived them closely, I listened to them.
But I want to tell you two.
It is a poor family, they have a 6 or 7-year-old child with mental disability.

They had dug a hole in their yard and buried the child every morning with his head sticking out before they left for work, and dug him up every afternoon when they returned from work.
I am visiting a social organization that truly embraces and cares for young people with severe disabilities.
Imagine that children, the youth, cannot even stay lying in their beds.
They are forced to tie them because their bodies cannot hold.
Until they are 23 or 24 years old, they can be hosted in some special facilities where there are truly heroic people who take care of them.

Well, you should know that these young people usually end up in Dafni.

When you are the recipient of all this scenery, you have two options.
You say I take all my feelings, open the closet, throw them inside, the more painful, the deeper, I close the closet, and never open it again.
And since I saw it, I must do something.
Even if I am wrong, because there is nothing worse in a society than indifference.
It is better to do something and do it wrong, than to do nothing at all, ever.
I want to do something but how will I do it? I am not a professional in this particular field.
I am not a social worker, I am not a psychologist, I am not an educator.
There, the answer came automatically: Yes, but I am a human being, and as a simple human being, I not only have the right, but also the duty to try to do something.

With the vision of a society where everyone, without exception, feels accepted and useful.

I had the opportunity and the chance to meet and speak with approximately 21,000 children, young people from general education, students, and young people with disabilities.
