Since the theme of this year's TEDx is Sharing Values, today we will talk about values.
The greatest and most enduring values that guide humanity today, which are: global peace, friendship, and cooperation among nations.

And since we are in Rhodes, in the Mediterranean, at the crossroads of three continents, we must see how we can emit Light from this tested part of the earth, in order to inspire hope in the expectations of the coming generations.

However, at this point, I would like to approach the subject through the more noble, in my humble opinion, undertaking that could be attempted, that of the "Revival of the Ancient Wonder of the Colossus," narrating, if you allow me, in a chronological way, through my personal experience and participation.
We are in the year 1999, and we are approaching the new Millennium.

A milestone date, in my opinion, was when the proposal for the creation of a millennium monument, that is, a millennium project, inspired ideologically by the ancient wonder of the Colossus, was put forward.
The goal of the project, at this particular moment in time, marking the New Millennium, was to turn the gaze of all humanity toward our island.

Not driven by grandiose ideas, but by creating a work of remarkable cultural contribution from Rhodes, which could simultaneously serve as a Center for spreading the timeless ideals of Greek civilization throughout the world.
Let us, however, look at what the proposal consists of, starting from its ideological content, which constitutes the summary of all that value material it should include.

The Colossus is the eternal myth that the city of Rhodes carries, and we attempt to restore its presence without, however, altering it by creating a sterile and anachronistic replica with archaeological extensions.

The philosophy and ambition of the project is to serve as an evolutionary ideological continuation of the ancient one, which was dedicated to the god Helios, peace, and freedom, to acquire a national character, and to be submitted as the Greek seal of the universal and timeless ideas of peace, friendship, and solidarity among nations, with the perspective of evolving into a global landmark and point of reference.

Thus, the vision of the project is as follows: Just as the ancient Colossus was built to pay tribute to the god Sun, peace, and freedom, the modern wonder, located at the crossroads of three continents, aspires to become a global symbol of peace and friendship among nations, and through the mental emission of light into human ideas and actions, to highlight Rhodes as a city-lighthouse, spreading messages of hope and optimism to the expectations of future generations.

How can we turn all these into action? The conception of the project must be based on the creation of a modern work of artistic expression and dimensions of an innovative technical achievement of optical and 21st-century design, which, however, must respect the measure, harmony, and the rich historical, culturally and architecturally significant environment of Rhodes.

This work is expected to emerge from the result of an open international competition, which will be selected by a local and international selection committee composed of outstanding personalities from the fields of politics, culture, literature, arts, sciences, and technology, and must be placed under the patronage of the country's highest authority, the President of the Republic, in order to ensure recognition, international prestige, and absolute transparency of the procedures.

At this point, we should think about where we will get the money, especially during such a severe economic crisis. In this direction, we propose that the project be self-funded through the mobilization of distinguished personalities, Ambassadors of the Idea, who will lead an International Fundraising Campaign that will collect money from sources such as grants from Greek and international foundations, subsidies from official bodies, international organizations, the European Union, UNESCO, support from regional and municipal authorities, while also foreseeing the use of donations from wealthy Greeks, compatriots in the diaspora, and philhellenes from all over the world, who would wish to contribute to the benefit of the realization of the idea.
What we must at all costs avoid is the commercialization of both the work and the idea.

And for this reason, we propose that all the funds that will be collected be reinvested toward the promotion of peace and our cultural heritage, through the creation of a Thematic Museum for the ancient Colossus and its history, as well as through the establishment of an international Mediterranean peace center, which could house an outstanding cultural multipurpose complex containing a conference center, exhibition spaces, theaters, concert halls, and be able to host international events and conferences of global significance.
To avoid being vague, I would like at this point to mention only a few examples of landmark works that have brought glory and admiration to the cities and countries that undertook them.
The New Acropolis Museum, but also many other marvels, which have created significant added value wherever they were built.
It would, however, be a great omission at this particular moment, in today's era, not to refer to and not to foresee the establishment of an Archaeological Research Center.

In order for the effort to remain healthy and sustainable for the upcoming generations of young scientists, it is proposed that a significant portion of the project's income be allocated to the establishment of an Archaeological Research Center, and even an academic Colossus chair, which would support the research and studies of archaeologists and researchers on issues that could concern the possible location, future findings, and in accordance with the latest scientific data.

What, however, will be the ultimate challenge for the project? The ultimate challenge is to establish the belief that in the long term, Rhodes can claim internationally the reputation of a "Neutral Island of Peace in the Mediterranean," a "Davos for resolving conflicts," and indeed, in the midst of an acute refugee crisis, and through the symbolic creation of the Colossus project, to maximize all the collateral but highly significant benefits.

Rhodes, with its multicultural identity and its outstanding geographical position, becomes an ideal venue for bridging ideas and cultures, establishing free dialogue, as well as promoting the ideals of peace, friendship, and solidarity among nations.

Such an initiative will have the potential to provide multiple benefits to Greece in terms of tourism, cultural, economic, social, and national aspects, and will be considered worthy of sending a message of the country's dynamic presence in international developments to the global community, thus confirming the evolutionary perspective of Greek civilization.
We firmly believe that this project is a matter for all the residents of Rhodes and all the Greeks.
It concerns one of the most valuable elements of our national identity, our cultural heritage, our history, our myths and legends.
It requires great care in handling it so that we embody it without touching upon its history and culture, but rather proving ourselves worthy successors of it.

At this point, therefore, we call upon: We call upon every resident of Rhodes, every Greek, and every citizen of this world who feels a friend to Greek ideals, to support us in realizing our vision: to offer to our Rhodes, to Greece, to Europe, to the Mediterranean, and to the rest of the world, a landmark work for the 21st century.
A global seal of the values of peace, unity, and cooperation among nations that will forever radiate light and hope into the inspirations and aspirations of future generations.
There is a scientific field that is very old and concentrates the distillation of human thought, at least regarding the way decisions should be made.
At the same time, it is a system that works well only when the person using it has decided to take a risk.
I will speak to you about courage and justice, and I will begin by making a bold distinction: laws—the existing legislation—are different from justice as a science.
Laws are the written expression of the rules established by the State.
Justice, however, is a much broader concept that includes the scientific rules of interpretation and application of laws.
Thus, interpretation and application largely determine the actual power of laws, the way justice is enforced by courts and other public authorities.
The science of law often dares to attack the laws of the State and can even go so far as to completely neutralize them.
Thus, parallel to the hegemonic establishment of law, we also have legal science, which functions as "opposition" to a "consensus" from which it is not bound.
The most characteristic case in which lawyers showed their teeth to power was a long time ago.
It was when the Byzantine emperor, Justinian, prohibited the interpretation of laws and did so through a law, in order to keep for himself the authority of the sole interpretation.
Naturally, the lawyers of the time overcame the prohibition by interpreting it.
Thus, legal scholars continued to interpret the laws, recognizing that the emperor's privilege of "authentic interpretation" could not restrict the development of scientific analysis.
In our time, justice can provide answers to important questions we have as a society.
If we accept that justice is a combination, then we can seek information and answers through its search engine.

There are answers that can be given by applying general principles included in the law, such as the principle of equal treatment, or the principle of free development of personality, the principle of hearing the arguments of both parties.

Through the mechanisms of law, we can identify methods ranging from conflict resolution and peaceful coexistence, to highly complex thinking mechanisms for the protection of the environment and for the development of creativity and art.

By applying legal rules, we can decide whether a specific piece of art is allowed to be presented to the public or not, as was the case with this work that was removed from the 2003 Cultural Olympiad and we still cannot find it on the internet in a clear image, only through reproductions in the press of that time.
Or we can answer whether an industrial unit meets the conditions of "sustainable development."
Sustainable development is not a political slogan but breaks down into specific rules, the implementation of which we can monitor in a measurable way.
Justice, of course, does not provide answers for everything; there are matters of aesthetics and personal choices that always remain in the realm of subjective judgment.

We very often consider justice as something we leave as a last resort, because we have associated it with all these things: the administration of justice, lawyers, courts, the money one must spend, the time one must devote, the long wait for a final court decision, the psychological cost, the anxiety, all these things for which every time we get to know a lawyer personally, we tell him that it is better if he remains unnecessary to us.
Justice is not a popular compass for making decisions, exactly because we associate it with all these negative consequences.

How can we then find justice without courts? And how can we avoid doing this, relying entirely on personal and subjective notions of "justice," that is, on the personal morality and individual value systems of each person, with which we may completely disagree as a society? Because the pursuit of justice beyond the courts can lead to vigilantism.

Since I know the law, am I allowed to take it into my own hands? Even in cases where vigilantism is a socially acceptable way of settling accounts, it is not acceptable that it constitutes a form of administering justice.
Feuds and vendettas are cases in which individuals themselves attempt to resolve their differences.

The difference between self-judgment and the administration of justice is that in the administration of justice, the response always comes from an independent third party, who first considers the arguments of the parties and then decides.
Thus, we cannot have justice if there is not this third party that interprets and applies the law.
But this would mean that we cannot have justice without courts!
Can we have justice without courts? In Crete, they have found a way.
The long tradition of self-judgment in Cretan societies led to the formation of an informal institution for out-of-court resolution of disputes.
In Crete, in order to avoid courts and vendettas, they turn to a respected fellow citizen, the honor of whom is recognized by all parties.
He is the person who will spend a lot of time with one side and the other, in order to resolve their dispute.
This mediation process is called "sasmos" and it is still in use today in Crete.
There are famous arbitrators who spend many hours and days on one side and the other to resolve differences that otherwise could have led even to bloodshed.
Bypassing the judge is an extremely bold choice.
It is, however, not a foreign option to the Greek administrative system.
Greek legislation already includes institutions similar to the Cretan "sasmo."

Since 2011, under the "Kallikratis" Program, in every municipality with a population of more than 20,000 residents, the municipal council selects a person of standing and experience, to whom it assigns the resolution of citizens' disputes with the municipal services.
This is the "Citizen and Business Supporter," and it is already operating in about 35 municipalities.
I was the Citizen and Business Advocate in the City of Athens from 2012 to 2014.

Hundreds of citizens then came to the mediation office we set up there, to resolve their disputes with the City of Athens, and through this process, justice was served in many cases without the citizens needing to go to court.
Thus, the Municipality avoided many court cases, and the citizens were able to obtain justice without costs and without major delays.
Normally, a Citizen and Business Advocate should exist in 161 municipalities.
These are the municipalities with more than 20,000 inhabitants.

The fact that it exists in only 32, of course, does not mean that the institution has failed, but that the local authorities dare not welcome the independent mediator who would resolve their disputes with citizens.

Long-term client relationships, suspicion regarding the degree of independence of mediators, and generally this indifference toward institutions and the law itself are the reasons why mayors and municipal councils deprive citizens of their right to extrajudicial resolution of their disputes.
In 2015, I was elected Regional Councillor for the Citizen and Business in the Attica Region.
In total, out of the 13 regions of the country, only 7 have elected a Citizen Advocate.
The others have not yet shown the required institutional courage to appoint their own Ombudsman.
This is now a way of resolving, out of court, disputes between citizens and public services.

What happens, however, when the difference is between two states? Beyond diplomacy and international courts, is there a way for justice to be found by an independent mediator in disputes between states? Mediation institutions do exist in international law.
Such an institution is the Mediation Committee established by UNESCO to resolve disputes concerning cultural goods that have been removed from their places of origin.

In 2013, Greece requested from UNESCO that this Committee examine the case of the Parthenon Marbles located in the British Museum, which would require the United Kingdom's agreement to refer the matter to mediation.
As is known, the United Kingdom rejected the invitation in March 2015 and did not dare to attend the mediation.

Let us think for a moment about this case: even if someone is opposed to the return of the Parthenon Marbles to Greece, it is certain that Elgin damaged this monument, vandalized the Acropolis, without ever being judged by a court as to whether the act itself was legal or illegal.
That is, Greece has not dared for many decades, perhaps even centuries, to refer to a court to have this act of Elgin judged, even in passing.
On the one hand, Greece is not going to court, and on the other hand, the United Kingdom does not dare to approach a mediation process.
Last summer, the older residents' association of Athens, the Athens Residents' Association, contacted me.
The members of this association are the descendants of the families who lived in Athens from the beginning of the 19th century.
That is, the families who lived around the Acropolis at the time when Elgin stripped the Parthenon of its sculptures.
These families founded the Athenians' Association in 1895, setting among other goals the pursuit of justice for the Marbles case.
These people entrusted me with their application to the European Court of Human Rights.
This is, of course, an extremely complex judicial undertaking, which most lawyers would likely be skeptical about.

However, having examined that any decision by the European Court would concern the relationship between a private association and the United Kingdom, without affecting Greece's sovereign rights over the sculptures, we decided, with very careful steps, to proceed with the application.
Thus, in September 2015, we filed our appeal in Strasbourg.

With this appeal, we request that it be recognized that the United Kingdom, by retaining the Parthenon sculptures on its territory and asserting that they were lawfully acquired by Lord Elgin, violates human rights related to the respect for cultural identity and the integrity of access to a universal heritage site.
Courage is an essential characteristic in a legal context and with regard to individual freedoms.

Consider how many bold decisions based on justice and on the principle of equal treatment have significantly changed the world in which we live; especially by naturally abolishing older laws, such as the decision, for example, to prohibit racial discrimination, the right to vote for women, and the recognition of the universal validity of human rights.
These decisions are decisions that in past times would have sounded completely insane.
When such decisions are made by courts to which a case is referred to serve as a "pilot," we legal professionals often speak of "judicial activism" or, more mildly, of "strategic litigation."
Imagine someone telling you ten years ago that two people of the same gender could form a civil union in Greece, the results of which are almost identical to those of a civil marriage.
But consider also how outdated the voices sound today that are opposed to this form of equality.

Some of our fellow citizens in 2009 turned to the European Court of Human Rights because, due to their sexual orientation, they had been excluded from their right to enter into a cohabitation agreement.
If they had not done so, it is very likely that even today Greece would still be one of the last countries not recognizing human and fundamental rights for same-sex couples.
The decision of the European Court was obvious to everyone who knows the law, although there were also opposing legal arguments.

The distance between knowing, understanding the violation of human rights, and appealing to the European Court of Human Rights was bridged by the courage of these people who went to Strasbourg and dared to exercise their rights against the state.
The thread connecting all these stories about activating justice is courage.

The lawyers who proved they are not bound by the emperor's ban, the Cretans who appeal to the "sashti" instead of the judge, the municipalities and regions that elect a Citizen Supporter and a Regional Supporter for the Citizen and the Business, the Athenians who demand justice in the case of the Parthenon Marbles, and our fellow citizens who won in the European Court the right to enter into a cohabitation agreement—all of them proved that they dare.
Because justice is a living organism that is not confined to courtrooms, and its doors are ready to open when some, with determination, dare to knock on them.

Since the issue had somehow disappeared from the media, I asked him, "Kyriakos, are the boats still coming? How is the situation?" And I remember distinctly the tone of his voice when he told me, "Come here and see for yourself, I don't have words to describe what is happening."

When I arrived there with my colleague, the American who did the sound for the documentary, we decided on the first day, without cameras, to go to the other end of the island, to see what was happening in Molivos, where the waves of migrants were arriving.

It was a one-and-a-half to two-hour route through the mountains of Lesvos, with turns, classic ones, and we were heading toward the beach, and suddenly, as we were going down to the beach, we saw about ten people wrapped in those blankets, the plastic, the characteristic ones, dripping wet and walking up toward the road.
It shocked me, it was the image I had been seeing for so long on the Internet.
Suddenly, someone opens my car door, a man, and pushes in a very old woman, who was purple, water was running everywhere, she was soaked to the bone, and she had almost lost consciousness.
And the man says to me, "Hospital, hospital."
I couldn't do anything, I was shaking and couldn't press the clutch in the car.
I'm ashamed to say it, but I couldn't cope, I couldn't drive, and I couldn't take her to the hospital.
Suddenly, what I had been reading about for so long, what I had been so informed about, became reality.
These people were on a thin line between life and death, and I saw the second side immediately.
Fortunately, another man came and told me there was an emergency aid station, so I could take the woman there, and I was fortunate because I think I wouldn't have managed to take her to the hospital.
I stayed there until it got dark, where the boats came almost every hour.

And indeed, I remember looking up at the sky and saying, "Where are the helicopters? Where is everyone?" Apart from five to ten volunteers and the coast guard, ten people in the sea, there was no one else.
Everyone knew and there was no one.
The next day, I met Kyriakos Papadopoulos for the first time.
He was a very low-key person, 43 years old with two little girls, who had a normal life before this crisis began.
He lived on a Greek island, a closed society.
Of course, he was informed and had his own opinions, and he confessed to me that what was happening didn't please him—the things he was hearing—and he didn't know who these people were.
Also, as I said before, she had no first-aid training, and she had never imagined that she would find herself in the middle of the biggest humanitarian crisis in recent years.
Stella still works at the Molivos cafe.
She confessed to me that when the first waves of immigrants started coming, she didn't like it at all.
He had never seen women with veils before.
She felt this thing like an invasion, because suddenly hundreds of people were coming, and it seemed strange to her.
On the evening of October 28, 2015, a very big shipwreck occurred, with hundreds of people in the sea.
Kyriakos Papadopoulos and the fishermen and the coast guard kept bringing out people, children, soaked to the bone, unconscious.

Fortunately, there was a doctor from Thessaloniki there entirely by chance, who had come for tourism, who managed to resuscitate all the children, turning them over and patting them on the back so that the water would come out of their lungs.
The whole village had come to the dock and was trying to do whatever it could.
Suddenly, Kyriakos came with another dose of people and started shouting, "Stella!"
"Stella!" and he pulled a pregnant woman out of the boat.
She imagined that Stella would help her as a woman.
Stella brought this Syrian woman into the café, who had been in the water for four hours, in the icy water, it was winter.
Of course, there were no doctors, so with various tricks using alcohol he tried to see if the baby would kick.
After hours of labor, the baby finally kicked, and when it did, the woman said, "My husband is in the wreck too."
Of course, many people had already been rescued, but there were still hundreds inside the sea.
And Stella tells her, write his name on a piece of paper and I will find him.
And indeed, Stella left the woman inside the cafe and went out, and for hours she searched for that man.
And he brought him to the cafe.
The couple looked at each other and hugged tightly, the man got down on one knee, and of course, Stella was deeply moved, as were everyone else who was there.
A few months ago, Stella sent me a photo.
Especially when we watch one-minute videos, or when we read news headlines, very naturally, because we all have work.
Sometimes, our feelings about a situation that is so far away turn into fear, and I consider this justified.
And when I did the "4.1 miles," I realized that the line between life and death is very thin.
I realized that we all experience it the same way: the fear, the death, the joy, the struggle, the survival—we all experience it the same, all the mothers and all the children.
I tried to do my job in the most honest way and simply observe people in order to understand the situation.
And what I realized in the end is that these two worlds, these two realities can be bridged, no matter what boundaries we construct.
I want to see raised hands from those of you who are fans of science fiction movies.
Are the rest of you ashamed? Come on, I am too!
Well, these movies are nice, but you'll agree with me that sometimes what they tell us is impossible, it's exaggerated.
After all, the expression "What you're telling me is like a science fiction movie" is not random.
They show us a humanoid robot, which, okay, comes from the future, but which listens, sees, understands what people are saying, what they want to do.
It has a little problem with irony, but by the end of the movie, it fixes that, and in fact, he even makes his own jokes.
Of course, someone with bad intentions might say that this particular robot has a slight acting problem, but we don't care about that.
What interests us is that the Terminator sees, hears, and understands.

Fast forwarding several years later, in this year's movie Ex Machina, the protagonist is named Ava, who is also a humanoid robot, incorporating intelligence and information from billions of people who use a hypothetical search engine called Blue Book.

The Blue Book is, one might say, an obvious play on words between Deep Blue, the first computing machine that defeated a chess world champion, and Facebook, the well-known social network, to which we all contribute our ideas, our dreams, our thoughts, and some photos of our favorite cats.
Imagine, then, a digital "mind," equipped with information and ideas from billions of people, which has artificial intelligence capabilities and can therefore decide, learn, and act on its own.
Of course, in the magical world of Hollywood, this is nothing new.
We have already seen it since the 1960s, from the HAL9000, who stars in the movie "2001: A Space Odyssey."
There, Arthur C. Clarke describes a computer from which we see only its characteristic red eye.
Nevertheless, his presence in the film is, I would say, leading.
HAL, therefore, can hear and understand what the astronauts want to do, and, most importantly, he begins to act when they want to disconnect him from the power supply.

In a characteristic moment of the movie, the two astronauts aboard the spaceship have retreated into the spacecraft's escape capsule to discuss exactly this, how they would get rid of HAL, thinking that inside there he couldn't hear them.
They forget, however, that HAL sees them through the windows and can read their lips.
We have, therefore, many wonderful things at our disposal, which we start implementing quite a few years later, around 1990, 95, and we give them the somewhat fishy name "Affective Computing."
Now, you might say, this name is like trying to put lemons and oranges together.

How can you have a concept like emotions, which by nature belongs to the field of psychology, expressed in the brain, in our body, and yet you want this thing not only to be understood by computers, but even recognized.
What we know about computers is that they do two things very well.
One is that they perform arithmetic operations extremely quickly, now performing trillions of operations per second, and that they have almost unlimited data storage capacity.
Well, and even though in recent years taking selfies helps us capture both significant and, mainly, trivial moments of our lives.
How can computers understand emotions, then? And yet, this last ability, the one with selfies, is the one that makes the difference.

Consider that, by taking photos, talking on our cell phones, and wearing a bunch of gadgets on our hands that record our heartbeats or how much we sweat, we're essentially using the ears and eyes of modern-day HALs that we have in our hands—or, many times, we even place them on our desks.
So, we have pictures that capture smiles, tears, depending on which team you are, accordingly.

You have phone calls that describe how our voice tone changes when we feel an emotion, and we also have the bracelets or smartwatches that describe our heart rate when we are waiting for our partner on the first date, or when that "magical" envelope arrives from the tax office, those seconds until we see whether the slip inside is green or pink.
The next step, then, for our computers to understand how we feel is, very simply, to take them by the hand and tell them.
Why, for all the money you spend on your new laptop, what will it do? Only calculations and data storage.

The famous American psychologist Paul Ekman said the following: there are some basic emotions, such as joy, sadness, and anger, which are perceived in the same way by all people, regardless of whether they live, for example, in Thessaloniki in 2015 or lived in Siberia 200 years ago.
Of course, this does not mean that these are the only emotions or that they are expressed in only one specific way.
It means, however, that these emotions are perceived with the same "label," with the same word.
And if you think about it a little, it seems logical.
A wide smile and open eyes are always perceived as "happiness," even by a newborn baby.
Or, correspondingly, when we squint our eyes and mouth, this is perceived as anger, regardless of our cultural background or any other acquired characteristics.

The good thing is that this whole process described by Ekman fits very well with something our computers can effectively learn to do, and that is to classify images or numbers into various categories that we describe to them.

This is done by converting whatever we give them into digital data and using a set of equations, and every time they "see" a new photograph, for example, or a new conversation, they slightly change the coefficients of these equations, so gradually, progressively, they learn to classify them into the category we tell them.
For example, if we show many pictures of smiling people to a computer, we consider that it can be trained to distinguish the emotion of happiness from pictures showing other emotions.
Since this process resembles what happens in the human brain, with neurons and the change in their structure, we have therefore named this machine learning technique a neural network.
We can, therefore, encode an image, convert it into digital data, and feed it into such a neural network.
A better and easier idea would be to see how different this expression shown in the photo is from a neutral face.
For example, the feeling of joy, the expression of joy, is encoded by slightly raising the lips, raising the eyebrows, and opening the eyes.

And so we believe that, if we give a lot of data to such a computer, to a neural network, it will learn to distinguish a person's happy expressions from other expressions, even if it has not seen them before.
Either it will stand out with a very lively smile, even if it has learned more solemn smiles.
It is, you see, a humanoid robot.
I often describe it as "a computer with hands and feet."
It can hear what we say, using the microphones it has on its head.
It can see with the cameras it has in front.
Not very effective, it's the truth.
So, in general, Naos can do anything that the computer you have in your office or your mobile phone can do.
Okay, you won't use it to play Call of Duty, but you can connect to the internet, download your emails, and ask Naoko to read them to you.
Or you can log on to Facebook and read from your "Wall" where your friends are and how they're getting along, without you.
Or you can ask him to start a Skype call with your grandmother, whom you haven't spoken to for a semester.

And if you prefer to do all this in a more conventional and familiar way, imagine Nao on the grandmother's sofa, watching their favorite Turkish series together, and the grandmother starting a Skype call with you.
Or in the evening, before a child goes to sleep, to read them a fairy tale, and to use the camera and the microphone it has to understand the laughter or crying and react accordingly.

Of course, I would say that the most important thing is not so much to understand a person's emotions when they are speaking on the phone or sitting in front of us, but rather what we will do with that understanding.

In the case of messages we send via mobile phones or posts we make on social networks, we are already able to use this information to prevent potential suicides, by searching for specific words that trigger an alert for the police authorities.

Or we can, and this is already being done experimentally, manage patients with depression by using this information again, or even how often they leave their home, when they meet with other people and where, using all of this along with the GPS of their mobile phone.

I would say that, in the case of emotions, what we could do is not so much to understand whether someone is happy or sad, but to use, for example, their smile or intense gaze, when they are trying a product or watching a lesson, to see if they are focused, or when they are driving on the road.
Is he watching the road, or is he ready to fall asleep, or is he looking for the mobile phone? And we should act accordingly.

Another thing we do systematically is what we call "opinion analysis," where we computationally read the posts we make on social networks, and thus we can estimate people's opinions about a product, a person, or an event, and use it for marketing purposes.
So, I would say that we have the ability to understand, by listening, seeing, and reading, what people are doing.
But at most, let us reach understanding.
The days, for example, of the Terminator, who roamed the streets and sowed panic, or of HAL, who sacrificed the astronauts to save his own module, are, I would say, very far away.

And how could it be otherwise, when we have great figures of our time, such as Stephen Hawking or Bill Gates, warning us that if artificial intelligence gets out of our control, the results will be catastrophic.
It will start making decisions that we will not be able to control.
I would say, the key is exactly this.
Machines may have more freedom in 30 years than they have now, but the key is exactly how much freedom we will grant them.

What can they do, when not only will they listen to our conversations, they will see where we are, but they will also, for example, have access to our medical records, or have access to our phone's GPS and know where we are.

And even if we are sure that machines won't intentionally do us harm, as Skynet wanted to do in the Terminator movies, how do we know that when they encounter a situation they have never faced before, they won't make a wrong decision that is destructive? In the case of robots, the author Asimov solved this problem, albeit rather philosophically, by formulating the three well-known laws of robotics.
That the robot will try to save the human, will not harm them, and then will try to save their belongings.
In real systems, what happens? I would say our main problem is that we ourselves, watching Hollywood movies, rush to see artificial intelligence mature.
We must always remember that artificial intelligence systems operate effectively only within well-defined frameworks.
We said before, they beat us at chess, where they exploit their capabilities to perform very fast calculations and store the necessary data.
They use data from weather satellites, from ground stations, and all the models they can create.
Even on the stock exchange, artificial intelligence systems are more effective than any human analyst.

When Lehman Brothers collapsed in America at the end of the previous decade and that domino effect began costing tens of billions of dollars to banks and markets, the only company that not only didn't lose, but actually profited, was Medallion.
Medallion is not based on experienced stock traders, but on scientists with PhDs, who use signal processing algorithms to analyze information and stock prices across all markets.
It seems, therefore, that we already have machines at our disposal that decide and act before us, on our behalf.
And okay, we wouldn't let them perform surgery, mainly because very few people would accept that.
We are already letting them go and they drive the metro trains, while in Trikala they are already testing urban buses, which navigate on their own, without a driver.

Aircraft? Would you board a plane if you were told that instead of a pilot in the driver's seat, there is a box with processors and memory? Have you ever considered that you might already be doing this? Perhaps our planes are simply navigating between points set by pilots in the flight plan, even before our journey begins? And if in that tragic Germanwings flight there had been, instead of the pilot, a computer system in the cockpit, which would not have allowed a sudden loss of altitude over a very high mountain, such as the French Alps, following some logical rules set by the Civil Aviation Authority, wouldn't this have protected us from the pilot's suicidal tendencies? Have we ever wondered whether, in the end, what we should fear more is not artificial intelligence, but natural stupidity? Thank you.
(Laughter) (Applause) We said, we must take the computers from their hands, we said it many times.

It is a great honor for me to be here, and I thank the organizers of TEDxAcademy for the wonderful event and for their invitation for me to share my thoughts with you, which I will obviously do in English.
I don't want to subject you to my Greek, and believe me, you wouldn't want it either.
What will I speak to you about? I will speak to you about Greece, digitalization, and the evolution that follows.
I work in market research, so I will present you with data.

I will show you market research data we have, and one of the sources I will use is our digital life research, which we conducted in more than 40 countries, including Greece, so we can make comparisons.
One of the reasons, if not the main reason, why I will present you with the data on the internet in Greece is because I want to move beyond the discussion that Greece is digitally absent.
As it would be like questioning whether the force of gravity is acting upon us or not.
Gravity acts upon us and digitally "We are present."
Unfortunately, the business world was significantly late in capitalizing on this particular opportunity, but this is a different presentation and I will not be focusing on this specific issue today.

We are going to examine how we act in the digital space, but I think it is particularly important to understand why we do what we do on the internet, as this will shed great light on the opportunity we have to change.
First, we will refer to the issue of trust.
We have research data regarding trust, and I believe it constitutes an important starting point at the moment this country's social fabric is breaking down.

Which institutions do we tend to trust and which not? We asked a nationally representative sample of people aged 15 and over to answer for each of these 19 institutions to what extent they tend to trust it or not.
The numbers in parentheses, the percentages, refer to the response "I tend to trust." There is a lot to see here.
We could discuss for many hours, but I will focus only on what is relevant to my speech today.
Firstly, there is only one entity that most people tend to trust.
This shows that in this country we suffer from a lack of trust.
And if you have the opinion that trust is decreasing, you are not wrong, it is indeed decreasing.
Last year, a decline in trust was observed for all these institutions in Greece, with the exception of radio, which remained stable, and the internet and the UN.
which increased, but it is important that the Internet rose more, in the confidence index, from 35% last year to 41%.
Of course, to be fair to the Internet, which I love, in this particular research there are participants over 55 years old, who represent 30% of the Greek population.
They not only do not use the Internet, but they might not even know what it is.

Therefore, to fully understand those who know what it is and use it, and how much they trust the internet, we had impressive news: The army lost its top position as the most trusted institution among the main internet users, who are 15-44 years old, which I will show you immediately is indeed the case, believe me.
We have observed that people aged 15-44 appear to trust the internet more than anything else.

Let's look at what we do on the internet, how we "live" while we are connected. Initially, internet penetration and usage in Greece is at 55%. This percentage refers to a representative national sample of people aged 15 and over, including the rural areas.
In Greece we have this, in other countries it is known as the digital divide.
We have particularly high penetration among the 15-24 age group, as I mentioned to you.
It decreases to 45-54 and completely disappears from the map for those aged 55 and above.
You can see them at the bottom of the slide with an 11% percentage.
Essentially, what we expect in Greece is the current generation to promote the adoption of the Internet among older age groups.
If we observe the Internet user population, a young, dynamic, and active part of our society is online, corresponding to 8 out of 10 users.
How much time do we spend on the internet? Well, in Greece, we spend 20 hours per week, which is one hour more than the global average.
From the moment we become internet users, we connect once a week, five days a week, and six out of ten connect at least once a day.
This fact coincides exactly with the global averages.
In Greece, it is interesting to note that the digital medium, once adopted, becomes the main source of information.
Thus, six out of ten connect at least once a day, and four out of ten listen to the radio and/or watch television.
In our daily activities, everything is done in the light of the sun.
At the bottom of the frame you will see the global averages.
As you can see, in all these different activities, except for product purchasing and transaction processing, which are online banking and bill payment, we are significantly above the global average.
On social media, here, 6 out of 10 of us are active on a daily basis and we spend most of our time on them.
We spend more than seven hours a week on social media.
We are sufficiently connected through social media.
We have an average of 198 contacts, over 65% of the world average, and for ages up to 20, the number of contacts jumps to 440.
We are not only connected but also a group of multi-voiced individuals, with 44% expressing positive or negative opinions about brands, 32% in parentheses being the global average.
40% of us actively seek to find what is said regarding brands, in contrast to the global average of 36%.

What, then, do we conclude from all of this? What is happening on the internet in Greece and in other countries, in simple terms, forces us to pay attention to what scientific studies of human behavior have been telling us for years, but which we have chosen to ignore.
Contrary to the perception of the 20th century, which is wrong since the 20th century ended 11 years ago, we know that humans are social animals, which we demonstrate on the Internet.
This is our nature; we are programmed to belong, we desire integration, and we thrive in complex social networks.

This essentially means two things: first, that social media represent the future of marketing, and it is no longer too late to decide whether your business will have a social presence on the internet, where people exist and discussions take place, whether you participate in them or not.
You only need to decide whether you will have a strategy for social media and what that strategy will be.
This presupposes a new model of thinking, as it requires upheavals.
I believe there is something deeper, evolutionary, that we can learn from the Internet and what we do there, which is the second lesson for us.
What is observed on the internet, both in Greece and the rest of the world, is the voluntary creation of natural hierarchies.
Together we solve problems, help each other, and build upon the contributions of others, just as others build upon our own.
We are taking selfless actions, not selfish but selfless, and indeed voluntarily.
However, in Western societies, the 20th-century perception, whose consequences we have endured, demands that human behavior, according to which we built our political systems.

Economically, legally, educational systems, in other words, all the systems in which we live, work, and learn, theoretically make us happy and progress, are based on the assumption that people are self-interested beings who want reward or punishment and require hierarchical control.
This perception is completely wrong, and I am not claiming that we are not self-interested, of course we are.
However, for the motives behind human actions, history does not stop here.
If we want to have the right story about human behavior, if we are to design systems that develop and reinforce cooperation as much as possible and unleash human potential rather than suppress it.

In a Greece where everything is collapsing and there is a need to create new systems, there arises the bright opportunity to redefine these systems—not by correcting the mistakes of the previous system, but by developing a new version that places the human being and the meaning of the concept of humanity at its center.
Essentially, we must listen with an open mind to what the sciences and the internet are trying to tell us about human existence.
Personal interest and cooperation can coexist; the same applies to profit and cooperation.
The way we see ourselves largely determines our development.
This is our one and only chance.

My name is Alkiviadis Panopoulou and today I will be talking to you about weight loss.

Obesity is a psychosomatic illness, which means that the obese person, the one who has a weight disorder, cannot recognize what they feel, their emotions.

Someone who wants to lose weight goes to a dietitian and even asks them to help them lose weight quickly.

Breakfast: 30 grams of corn flakes, a glass of milk, and a piece of fruit, or a toast made of two slices of toast bread, 30 grams each, 30 grams of boiled sausage, 30 grams of light cheese, and an orange juice.

Evening: 60 grams of honeycomb or two tablespoons of cajeta with two tablespoons of sicalia flour or 30 grams of sicalia bread with two cups of vegetables and two teaspoons of oil.
The overweight person then takes their diet, the list with all this information about vitamins, healthy foods, harmful ones, and goes home determined and starts their condemnation.

"I'm useless, and once again I messed everything up, I didn't accomplish anything, and I lack self-control, willpower, and self-discipline."
Or even less food, 80 grams, because everything is a matter of caloric balance.
Bulimia, diet, I lose weight, I gain weight, bulimia, I get discouraged.
This is what was happening in my own office during the first 10 to 12 years.
The scene was as follows: On one side were the overweight people, with their obsession with food and overeating, and with diets.
And on the other hand, it was me, and I had also developed a mania.

Finally, after observing all of this, I decided that since we always ran into an obstacle, and this obstacle was called binge eating, their binges made them gain weight, their binges ruined their diet, their binges emphasized their inability to maintain the diet, I started paying attention to the binges.
Fortunately, I had developed a passion for this job, and I also had a great many books to study.
There was a lot of knowledge I could acquire.
The first change I made is that I allowed binges.
Not only did I allow and legitimize them, but I even encouraged them to maintain their binges throughout their weight loss.
(Laughter) I realized that bulimia was the only truth that could help the person who had a weight disorder.
And here's why: "When the baby is born and comes into the world frightened, it is afraid and cries, it is hungry and cries, and the mother comes and takes it into her arms to feed it.
That's where the first connection between food and our emotions is made.
At that moment, the mother's love flows, her tenderness, her affection, her sense of security, and eye contact.
Thus, the full, calm, content, relaxed baby is taken by sleep into that perfect embrace.
Growing up, this connection between food and our emotions continues.
Parties, promotions, college admissions? Parties with nice tables, delicious food, drinks, and desserts.
On television, love or romantic disappointment is advertised with chocolate.
Even after death, after the funeral, there is a table.
So, this connection between food and our emotions is throughout our entire lives.

In adult life, then, a person who has a weight disorder, when faced with stress factors, stressful situations, the emotions that arise, because he or she has not learned to recognize them, reactively and unconsciously, what is the memory? Overeating emerges.
What is polyphagy? Simply put, it is a vehicle that one gets on and takes them back, going back and forth, during that period of absolute hedonism.
It is not a coincidence that after a big, delicious meal, we feel this drowsiness.
So, I forbade them from stopping the binges because they were the only anesthetic, calming, relaxing thing that a person with an eating disorder has.
The second reversal I made is that I prohibited the withholding of food.
Whether in terms of quantity - I work with portions, restaurant portions are types - or in terms of taste.
From one side, may it give you nourishment, energy, health in extension, you live from the food.
It's normal to get hungry, to feel your stomach pulling, and to wonder what delicious meal you'll eat to enjoy yourself.

It's not normal to not want fried potatoes.
They eat portions, because the starting point is that it's unnatural for anyone to go on a diet, especially a restrictive one, so when they succeed, they become a hero.

The third reversal I made is that I discovered that weight loss does not have two stages.

It has been four years, one after the other, where the person with a weight disorder has the great support to take as much time as needed for each stage, where in the first stage, they observe, see, become aware, feel, experience their binges, confront them, and deal with them.
In the second stage, there, having already developed some resistance to the idea that it does not commit murder, because it eats, it can and forms an opinion.

In that second stage, you usually see with great joy that they don't diet, skip meals, or are late for appointments.
When a person reaches the third stage, there, they have the ability to deal with the organic part.
There he himself asks now, to learn about the usefulness of vitamins because there he can act.

In this month's Vanity Fair, a long, elaborate article about Greece was presented.
The article was a product of observations and interviews, and I can say that it was extremely accurate, correct.
And of course detailed, because it started from an interview with the Minister of Finance, moved on to discussions with politicians, tax officials, and ended with ordinary citizens.
In this sense, therefore, it was both well-founded, and the intuition and knowledge of the person who wrote it were correct.

Very briefly, since the article was 25 pages, it said that Greece is a kind of third-world country with the worst education in Europe, a deteriorating healthcare system, a corrupt state that is also a corrupter at the same time, while the people are warm, friendly, smart, interesting, to the point of creating the impression that they are a great people, the Greeks.
However, what is characteristic in their behavior is that there is a huge lack of trust between one another.
A huge lack of trust that keeps us closed in, or at least among ourselves.
Suspicions arise constantly when a friend or an acquaintance of ours is affected.
And certainly, characteristic in behavior is lying, it is deception, it is theft, behaviors that undermine every notion of real public life.
The author, Michael Lewis, was thus interested in highlighting precisely the inability to create real public life in Greece.
Everyone works for themselves in an economy that is basically state-controlled, while, and this refers to the issues of Vatopaidi, the monks in the monasteries of Mount Athos live in Byzantine times.
Intense individualism, combined with a banking edge, equals moral collapse in recent years, but also long before that, in Greece.
In this sense, it points out that dealing with the economic issue is a matter of cultural change and not just an accounting issue.

And certainly, the extension of the issue is that in this closed economy and monastic-like society, Greeks are passive, as if they do not want to have their autonomy, they always blame others, and of course the question arises whether technically it was possible to reverse the situation, whether there was technically, practically, the possibility, he said, did the Greeks have the internal strength to do it? To rise up, to realize this potential? Or is this, he says, impossible since they are a sum of units that seek their own interest at the expense of the common good? And, of course, there he concludes by saying that, given the refusal to change the political system, can public life be reborn? This is in very short, these are the author's thoughts.
He avoids, or at least does not have the willingness and is unable, to proceed with a diagnosis.
We could, in a way, supplement what he says in order to perhaps more deeply understand the problems that he reveals with such precision and eloquence.
Through what he describes, we can marvelously conclude that our structural characteristic is partiality.
Particularism, in the sense that we are always seeking the particular.
Characteristic is partiality, that is, what makes it very easy for us to clean our homes thoroughly but to throw the trash into the street.
Thus, the narrowness of our good living nullifies the universality of public cleanliness.

The element, therefore, is partiality, and the explanation we could give for this partiality, or at least part of the explanation, has to do, I think, with the fact that our mental, our spiritual world is dominated by emotion.
Only an extremely emotional people or person can experience partiality so intensely in their thoughts and actions.

And it is very important to note here, to emphasize, that a characteristic of our culture is this intense emotional experience, and that is because after the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, there was no kind of Renaissance in Greece that would allow the cultivation of logic, and at the same time, an insistence on religious experiences led to the cultivation of an emotional realization of the absolute, which has remained in our lives and now dominates our souls and is a characteristic element in our choices.
Of course, this concept, let's say, this feeling of neglecting logical principles is also a neglect of the concept of duty at all times.
It narrows the boundaries of broader behavior, leads to the predominance of the pleasant over, say, the necessary or the right, and mainly leads further to a childish mentality.

We have something childish within us, we have a childishness, a childishness similar to all the cultures of minors, as well as all the phases of a minor human's life, which certainly leads us to an inability to become autonomous.

In this sense, dependence on the broader whole, every time we speak of family or a small local society, is a deep dependence, and this, of course, obliges us to an acknowledgment of identity—we take ourselves as small children from others, and not from within ourselves, as is the case with individuals of an advanced culture, an advanced civilization.
This means the following: We can have a public life with sufficiently high legally guaranteed democratic standards, but it is not certain that we can have a democratic culture.
The reason we are going through an acute crisis today is because during the last 35 years we had legal guarantees of democracy but we did not have a democratic culture.
We had, that is, a culture of rights and not of obligations.
In this sense, it was very easy to erode, to dismantle an entire structure while we were constantly protesting against it.
That is, while we were praising it and demanding it, at that very moment we were dissolving it, precisely because we were not moving toward such a dimension.

And of course, it goes without saying that the clientelist system is based precisely on the lack of this democratic culture, which in reality, essentially, complements and completes the legislative framework.

In other words, I mean to say that the element of evaluative, value-based criteria was and still remains an Achilles' heel that we are paying dearly for and will continue to pay dearly for a long time.
The interesting thing is that while we live and consume in this partiality, we are a people who claim universal realities and universal values on our own behalf.
By the way, I note here that we do not cease to be an Eastern people.
We are an Eastern people, precisely because we are a people of emotion.
Only the peoples of the West can combine the mind, the word with feeling.
We, the peoples of the East, live our feeling and eschatologically claim its verification.
This universality of our Enlightenment, with the absence of individual responsibility for things, always manifests itself in certain characteristic forms.
That is, with forms that, through ideas and great myths, do not have particular durations but have great intensities.
We know very well who the great national myths were.
It was the Great Idea that collapsed in '22, a myth, the European Idea, which we saw being expressed and triumphantly demonstrated in the Olympics and through the volunteers.
And certainly, it is a sense of time rooted in the present, and precisely because it is rooted in the present, it hates any form of duration.
And for this reason, the need for gratitude claims dimensions of eternity within us, although it is always the gratitude of the moment.
With the consequences we know, that is, with every absence of thought for the consequences.
This is very characteristic of our spiritual life.
But it is also characteristic to resort to such metaphysical expectations in order to better understand our behavior.

Can the Memorandum scare us and make us obedient, but if someone says that a Saint appeared in his dreams and told us to collect salt and I don't know what, flour, this thing can overturn all the fears that are in operation and destroy the entire obedience to the Memorandum that people who are currently in power try so hard to maintain.
I want to say, it is very important to see where the metaphysical tripod is in things.
Therefore, we have a reality that has such a character, and together with the American, we can also ask ourselves what kind of exit today's Greece could claim and pursue.
What is the interest and perhaps the positive aspect within the current crisis? Because anyway, no matter how bad things may be, there is always a horizon with light.
As bright as they may be, darkness waits in the depths.
It is interesting to observe and think carefully that today we are going through the phase of the final sealing of the great myths.
We are ending now, 80 years from 1840 to '22.
Eighty years was the Great Idea, and another so many, 90 years, is the European Idea, which had to be established in order to fill the void left by the Asia Minor catastrophe.
Now, we are at the end of the great myths of Greece.
Which means, for the first time under real conditions, to look each other straight in the eye.

It is the first time in our recent history that the great mythic systems can no longer function "deterrently," as I would say—because the "deterrent" would be for us to look at ourselves in the mirror.

For the first time, then, the mythical systems that are collapsing can no longer function "deterrently," so now the people and individuals are called upon to take the matter of their future into their own hands with a completely different logic.
A logic that certainly presupposes education, presupposes initiatives of this kind, which are very important.
But above all, it requires, at the individual level, at the micro-level of mindsets and behaviors, changes and initiatives that could be particularly useful and truly effective.
That is, one must now think in terms of synthetic approaches to things.
Openness, not fearful reactions toward realities we are called upon to face today.

Furthermore, self-actualization options through a real relationship with ourselves and others, in a new condition, which is the current condition imposing on people a different perception of interest.
Perception of self-interest within the common good.
